column6:
• Students need access to water.
• Label the small plastic container (16 ounce) with a 1. Fill the measuring cup to the 8 fluid ounce mark twice, pour the water into this container. Use black permanent marker, mark a line around the outside of the container. This is one pint.
• Label the medium plastic container (32 ounce) with a 2. Fill the 1 container with water twice and pour the water into this container. Using the black permanent marker, make a line around the outside of the container. This is one quart.
• Label the large plastic container (128 ounce) with a 3. Fill the 2 containers with water four times and pour the water into this container. Using the black permanent marker, make a line around the outside of the container. This is one gallon.
• Label the 30 ml graduated beaker with a 4. Fill the measuring cup to the one-ounce mark and pour the water into this container. Using the permanent black marker, make a ring around the outside of the beaker. This is one ounce.
• Fill the measuring cup to the 250 ml mark. Pour the water into the clear liter bottle. Repeat this process four times. Using the permanent black marker, make a line around the outside of the bottle. This is one liter.
• The students will need two pitchers of colored water. Fill both pitchers with water and add two tablets of the same color to both. After the tablets have dissolved, stir the water with the serving spoon.

column6:
It will take about 48 hours to prepare the eggs, so begin on Day 3 of the rotation. To prepare the naked eggs, do the following:
1. Use one flat, covered Rubbermaid container to prepare the eggs.
2. Fill the container about half full of vinegar, and carefully place the eggs inside.
3. Cover the container and place it in the refrigerator. 2. Let the eggs sit for two days. Open the container, pour out the vinegar and dissolved eggshell, and rinse the eggs. By this time, the shells should be completely dissolved. If particles of shell remain, either peel them off carefully or (if time permits) add more vinegar and soak them another day.
4. Before each class period, fill a plastic Solo cup half-full of tap water, carefully place two naked eggs in it, and place it at the workstation. Caution students to handle the eggs carefully.
5. If the eggs are handled carefully, they shouldn't break. It's suggested that the students make extra eggs to ensure the activity can be completed.

o Create 12 columns of 5 LEGO blocks.
o Place the bottom baseplate in front of you. This will be the first level of your tower.
o Add one column of LEGO blocks on each corner of the baseplate.
o Place a baseplate on top of the four columns.
o Add one column of LEGO blocks to each corner of the second baseplate.
o Place a baseplate on top of the four columns. o Add one column of LEGO blocks to each corner of the third baseplate.
o Place a baseplate on top of the four columns to make the top of your tower.
• Cut two pieces of Velcro ("hook" side) approximately four inches in length and two pieces of Velcro ("loop" side) approximately four inches in length.

column6: During this session, students are instructed to take a Lab Safety Contract home, read and complete the contract with their parent or guardian, and return the completed contract to you, the instructor, as soon as possible. It is recommended that students NOT be allowed to operate the torch or the lighter unless a contract has been returned. You may choose to operate these items even if students return a Safety Contract. Also, the pulse glass used in this session will break if it is squeezed too hard. Students are instructed to merely cover it with their hand, not squeeze it. The liquid inside is colored ethyl alcohol, so if it does break, it will not harm them. If you have concerns about students breaking the pulse glass, you may remove it from the workstation and either ask students to skip pages 110-112, or have them call you to demonstrate the action of the pulse glass. 4,000 mL of tap water is needed. You can save the glass plate and have the students determine its hardness as an enrichment activity. The penny and nail will have limited life expectancies of two to four rotations. When they become extremely scratched, it is difficult to tell whether the scratch was there before or after the hardness test was performed. For this reason, scratched nails and pennies will need to be replaced. The minerals contained in the kit can break if dropped or abused. Students are instructed to scratch the materials with the minerals, not to scratch the minerals with the materials.

column5: Digital scale, foam plate, masking tape and dispenser, dropper pipette, funnel, wash bottle filled with tap water, numbered soil sample, marker, soil texture unit (stand, graduated tubes, and caps), "Soil Texture" worksheet, mortar and pestle, stopwatch, resealable plastic bag, handled beaker
column6: During this session, students will be texturing one of the eight different soil samples. Sample Numbers 2, 4, and 7 will never be selected for the students to texture. Peat and muck are not actual soil textural classes but are composed entirely of organic matter. Sandy soil is simply too easy to texture. Starting this session on time is critical, because the texture by separation method is accomplished by letting soil particles settle out of a suspension for a specific amount of time. It is critical that Tube B be poured into Tube C before the students leave class. If less than the prescribed time of 20 minutes passes before doing so, the accuracy of the results may be compromised, depending on the soil sample being textured. Students are instructed to take the mass of the soil and container before starting the activity. In Session 7, after the soil has dried and been recovered, students should return it to its container and again take its mass. Ideally, students should return the same amount of soil to the container that they had originally. Any rock or debris found in the sample after taking the beginning mass can be removed only with the instructor's permission and after its mass has been taken. A small amount of soil will be lost through residues on the equipment, but this amount should be very small. The smaller the better. Recovery of soil is not necessary with soil samples other than those provided with the Module. There is nothing wrong with introducing local soil samples into this activity. The key is that you must know the texture of the soils the students are texturing, so that their results can be evaluated. You should be able to follow the texture by separation procedure in Session 3 and the texture by feel method in the Enrichments and arrive at the same texture for any one soil sample. If soil samples become accidentally spilled, you could then replace them with local soil samples. The Texture by Feel Enrichment offers another way of texturing soils, and these two methods can be compared if the students are able to complete this enrichment. However, the texture by feel method will take a complete class period.

column6: Students must start this session on time and proceed quickly. This session deals with the chemical properties of soil. During this session, students will be required to use a soil sample other than those provided with this Module. You will need to provide a sample for the students to use or instruct students to bring a sample from home. Each class hour will require a soil sample roughly equivalent to what is found in the jars of different textural classes. Resealable bags make good soil containers. With the use of a soil test kit, students will determine pH, nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium levels of a soil sample. Students must refer to the Soils Student Module Notebook in order to complete all the questions on the "Soil Fertility" worksheet. Though portions of the students' Performance Assessment relate to the "Soil Fertility" worksheet, you may choose to defer those assessment questions until a later time and instead allow the students to obtain the needed information during class and complete the worksheet at home. Distilled water will need to be provided from your classroom stock. Only distilled water should ever touch the tubes in the soil test kit. The use of tap water will produce inaccurate test results. Safety goggles and plastic gloves should be worn. Students are instructed to discard the wastewater and chemicals outside because they will contain soil and could damage indoor plumbing.

column5: Light-sensitive thinking putty, microwave lid, mug, dinner plate, rolling pin, plastic wrap, oven mitts, ruler, microwave oven, source of water
column6: In this session students will measure the speed of light using light-sensitive Thinking Putty and the microwave oven. Instruct students to remove the turntable from the microwave and heat a flattened piece of Thinking Putty for several seconds until measurable "hot spots" appear on it. The distance between the centers of two hot spots is the length of one-half of a microwave. They will substitute this measurement into a formula to calculate the speed of light. You may need to remind students to pay attention to the videos and follow them exactly. Failure to follow proper procedure will prevent them from obtaining measurements and doing the calculations. For example: If they fail to remove the turntable, there will be no distinct hot spots. If they leave the putty in the microwave too long, the hot spots will run together. If they do not measure between the centers of the hot spots, the wavelength measurement will be inaccurate. A second pound of Thinking Putty is included in the inventory. This can be used if students fail to follow instructions and heat their putty too long, so the hot spots run together. It will also be useful if you have several classes, one right after another. If you alternate the two pieces of putty, they will have more time to cool back to room temperature before the next use.

column6: In this session students will make a paper-and-pencil scatter plot showing star temperatures versus luminosities. They will plot the 25 stars in their set of star cards. This is not a linear plot, so they will have to estimate the placement of the points; they should be reassured that it's okay to estimate and that exact placement (while preferable) is not essential for their purpose. Their final scatter plot should resemble the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram that they will study later in the session. Also, plotting the star card information on the graph is time-consuming. If they take too long, they will not finish the session. They are instructed in a Tip that, if time is short, they should plot only one or two stars from each color group. They should be reminded of this. However, the more stars they can plot, the more realistic their plot will be (the more it will resemble the Hertzsprung-Russell Diagram).
